chunk_summary: This section discusses malignancy, trauma, vascular, and inflammatory mimics of bone infections, emphasizing red flag features for differential diagnosis in pediatric cases.
learning_objectives: Identify malignancy-related symptoms that mimic bone infections, such as night pain and weight loss.
Differentiate between vaso-occlusive bone infarction and osteomyelitis in children with sickle cell disease.
Recognize red flag features like persistent fever, rising CRP, or destructive imaging for reassessment of diagnosis.
Understand the role of repeat imaging and tissue diagnosis in cases with atypical progression.
key_facts: Malignancy mimics include leukemia, lymphoma, Ewing sarcoma, and osteosarcoma, presenting with limp, bony tenderness, or low-grade fever.
Red flags include fever persisting beyond 3-4 days of therapy, rising CRP, or destructive imaging crossing anatomic boundaries.
Children with sickle cell disease may experience vaso-occlusive bone infarction, which differs from osteomyelitis by diffuse pain and less swelling.
Marked limb swelling disproportionate to tenderness may indicate deep venous thrombosis rather than infection.
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis evolves less explosively than septic arthritis and produces joint stiffness and pain.
reference_quote: Clues that favor malignancy include night pain, weight loss, pallor, bruising, generalized lymphadenopathy, multifocal pain, cytopenias, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, or pain that fails to improve after appropriately chosen antibiotics. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Inflammatory and vascular conditions mimicking infections
